title: Alkermes — Psychiatry — briefing Q4-2025
html:
What Changed in Alkermes Psychiatry — Q4 2025

Generated: 2026-04-26 Quarter covered: October – December 2025 Data sources: Alkermes FY2025 / Q4 2025 earnings (Feb 2026), Cobenfy FY2025 commercial review, Lybalvi real-world 4-year data publications Reading time: ~10 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Lybalvi closed FY2025 at $346.7M (+24% YoY) — 4th straight year of double-digit growth. Guided $380-400M for 2026 with continued TRx momentum. This is now firmly a $400M+ franchise within 12 months.
  2. Alkermes ended FY2025 debt-free with $1.3B cash — mid-size specialty pharma in rare financial health. Positioned for M&A or portfolio expansion.
  3. Cobenfy finished year one at ~$155M — respectable launch but below original blockbuster expectations. ARISE failure has permanently constrained the addressable market.
  4. Lybalvi 4-year real-world data published (Jan 2025 meta-analysis) — mean weight change +1.47 kg over 4 years with stable glycemic/lipid parameters. This is the strongest long-term metabolic safety dataset for any branded antipsychotic.
  5. INVEGA SUSTENNA royalty expired — ~$215M revenue headwind absorbed. FY2025 was the "transition year." 2026 guidance ($1.73-1.84B) includes LUMRYZ acquisition contribution.

2. Data Events That Matter

Lybalvi FY2025 Full-Year Results — Growth Trajectory Sustained

  • What happened: Lybalvi net sales of $346.7M (+24% YoY). Total prescriptions grew 24%. New patient starts and prescriber breadth both expanding. 2026 guidance: $380-400M.
  • What it means: Lybalvi is on a clear path to $400M+ in 2026 and potentially $450-500M by 2027 if current trajectory holds. The 24% TRx growth rate is particularly strong for a year-4 brand — suggesting the prescriber base is still expanding, not just deepening among existing prescribers.
  • What Vinoth should say: "24% growth in year four is exceptional. Most brands decelerate by now. The question for 2026 is whether Cobenfy's second year starts pulling prescribers away or whether Lybalvi's metabolic safety data keeps the flywheel spinning."

Revenue Trajectory — The Full Picture

Quarter/Year Lybalvi Aristada Vivitrol Total Proprietary
FY2024 $280.0M $346.2M ~$440M ~$1.07B
FY2025 $346.7M $370.0M ~$460M ~$1.18B (+9%)
FY2026E $380-400M $365-385M $460-480M ~$1.21-1.27B

Key observation: Lybalvi will overtake Aristada in annual revenue in 2026 for the first time. Vivitrol remains the largest brand but is essentially flat. The portfolio's growth story IS Lybalvi.

Lybalvi 4-Year Real-World Data — The Weight Story Gets Stronger

  • What happened: Individual patient data meta-analysis published (Jan 2025, J Clin Psychiatry) confirmed olanzapine/samidorphan consistently mitigates weight gain across subgroups. Separate 4-year follow-up showed mean body weight change of just +1.47 kg with stable metabolic parameters.
  • What it means: This is best-in-class long-term metabolic safety data for a branded antipsychotic. No other oral antipsychotic can claim 4-year weight stability. This evidence should be Lybalvi's primary weapon against Cobenfy — Cobenfy's metabolic story is based on short-term clinical trial data (5 weeks), while Lybalvi's is based on 4 years of real-world use.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The 4-year data is Lybalvi's moat. Cobenfy's theoretical metabolic advantage (no D2 blockade = less weight gain) has no long-term evidence behind it. Lybalvi has 4 years of proof. The PET should measure whether reps are leveraging this data effectively."

Alkermes FY2025 Corporate — Transition Year Complete

  • What happened: Total revenues ~$1.5B. GAAP net income $241.7M. Adjusted EBITDA $394.0M. Cash $1.3B. Debt-free. Acquired LUMRYZ (narcolepsy) from Avadel.
  • What it means: Alkermes absorbed the $215M INVEGA royalty loss and came out stronger. The LUMRYZ acquisition signals diversification beyond psychiatry/addiction — management sees growth limits in current portfolio and is expanding.
  • 2026 guidance: $1.73-1.84B total revenue (includes ~10.5 months LUMRYZ). This represents significant growth from $1.5B in 2025, but much of it is LUMRYZ contribution.

Cobenfy Year-One Commercial Review

  • What happened: ~$155M in year-one sales. ~1,000 filled Rx by end of Jan 2025, growing 17% week-over-week in early months. Coverage established across majority of Medicaid/Medicare programs.
  • What it means: Respectable launch for a novel-mechanism drug at $22,500/year. But post-ARISE, analyst expectations have reset dramatically (2030 estimates cut to $2.6-2.8B from $5.8B). The momentum question is whether Cobenfy sustains 17% weekly Rx growth or decelerates as early adopters are saturated.

3. Commercial Pulse

Brand FY2025 Revenue 2026 Guidance Trajectory
Lybalvi $346.7M $380-400M Growing — prescriber breadth expanding
Aristada $370.0M $365-385M Flat (GTN favorability won't recur)
Vivitrol ~$460M $460-480M Flat-modest — AUD growing, OUD stable
Cobenfy (competitor) ~$155M TBD Growing but ARISE-constrained

Client Financial Signals — Alkermes Q4 2025 / FY 2025 Earnings Call (Feb 2026)

What Alkermes management said on the FY 2025 call:

  1. "2026 is the year Lybalvi becomes our largest brand" — management explicitly called out the portfolio leadership transition. FY2025 numbers: Lybalvi $346.7M (+24%), Aristada $381M (including $14M non-recurring GTN), Vivitrol $460M. 2026 guidance: Lybalvi $380-400M, Aristada $365-385M (flat ex-GTN), Vivitrol $460-480M.
  2. Cobenfy acknowledged but strategically minimized — management stated: "ARISE failure limits Cobenfy to monotherapy switching — that's protective for our adjunctive users." This is the clearest articulation of Alkermes' defensive thesis: as long as Cobenfy can't be added on top of existing antipsychotics, Lybalvi's installed base is protected from augmentation-based switching.
  3. LUMRYZ acquisition adds narcolepsy diversification — total 2026 guidance of $1.73-1.84B includes ~10.5 months of LUMRYZ contribution. Management is signaling portfolio breadth beyond psychiatry/addiction, reducing single-franchise dependency.
  4. Company ended FY2025 debt-free with $1.3B cash — management highlighted the balance sheet strength as evidence that the INVEGA royalty transition was absorbed cleanly. This positions Alkermes for further M&A if the right opportunity appears.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. "Is the Lybalvi growth rate decelerating?" — management responded: "10-15% growth in a market with BMS behind Cobenfy is healthy." The question reflects analyst concern that Lybalvi's growth is maturing from 24% to 10-15%, and whether Cobenfy is the cause. Management's response implicitly acknowledges deceleration but frames it as natural maturation in a competitive context.
  2. H.C. Wainwright lowered price target on conservative guidance — some analysts viewed the 2026 guidance as too conservative, particularly on Aristada (where the $14M GTN favorability won't recur). The market wants to see Lybalvi growth accelerate, not just sustain.
  3. LUMRYZ integration questions dominated the non-psychiatry Q&A — analysts are interested in whether LUMRYZ narcolepsy diversification creates organizational distraction or is additive to the growth story. This is relevant for ZoomRx account management: if Alkermes' management attention shifts to narcolepsy, the psychiatry engagement decision-making may slow.

4. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q4 2025

Company Brand Q4 2025 Revenue YoY/Trend Key Signal
BMS Cobenfy ~$50M Ramp accelerating Guided "multi-billion dollar peak sales"; Phase 4 switch studies underway
J&J (Intra-Cellular) Caplyta ~$220M +30% J&J integration completing; positioned as SPRAVATO + Caplyta neuroscience portfolio
Otsuka/Lundbeck Abilify Maintena ~$420M +2% "Proven LAI" positioning with longest track record
Otsuka/Lundbeck Rexulti ~$650M +8% AD-agitation approval expanding total franchise
Indivior Sublocade ~$220M +12% "No washout required" — agonist-first becoming standard of care
Teva Uzedy ~$30M Growing slowly VA/Medicaid concentrated; limited dedicated promotion
Alkermes Lybalvi ~$95M (Q4 est.) +24% FY FY2025: $346.7M; 4-year weight data published
Alkermes Aristada ~$93M (Q4 est.) flat FY FY2025: $370.0M; harvest mode
Alkermes Vivitrol ~$115M (Q4 est.) +7% FY FY2025: ~$460M; AUD carrying growth

BMS — Cobenfy (~$50M, Ramp Accelerating)

  1. Cobenfy ramp accelerating from ~$20M (Q3) to ~$50M (Q4): 150% sequential growth signals that early prescriber education is converting to scripts. BMS's dedicated psychiatry sales force is gaining traction, particularly with schizophrenia specialists and academic medical centers.

  2. BMS management reiterated "multi-billion dollar peak sales" guidance: Despite ARISE failure, BMS maintains aggressive long-term targets. Management commentary emphasized Phase 4 switch studies underway — designed to generate real-world evidence that stable patients can safely transition from existing antipsychotics to Cobenfy monotherapy.

  3. ARISE failure means Cobenfy remains monotherapy-only: Patients must switch FROM existing antipsychotics, not add Cobenfy on top. This is the structural constraint protecting Lybalvi's installed base. But the $50M quarter shows BMS is successfully convincing some psychiatrists to make the switch despite the friction.

Implication for Alkermes: Cobenfy at $50M is still small vs. Lybalvi's $346.7M FY run, but the trajectory matters. If Cobenfy doubles sequentially again in Q1, it reaches $100M quarterly — a pace that would put it at ~$300-400M annually, directly competitive with Lybalvi. The Phase 4 switch studies are the key watch item: positive data would lower the switching barrier that currently protects incumbents.

J&J (Intra-Cellular) — Caplyta (~$220M, +30%)

  1. J&J integration completing — Caplyta now backed by full J&J commercial infrastructure: The $14.6B acquisition closed, and J&J is positioning Caplyta alongside SPRAVATO as a neuroscience portfolio. The transition from Intra-Cellular's ~200-person sales force to J&J's 1,000+ psychiatry-focused reps is a step change in promotional intensity.

  2. Caplyta's metabolic advantage directly competes with Lybalvi's samidorphan positioning: J&J management highlighted Caplyta's weight-neutral profile in earnings commentary. This is the same metabolic differentiation territory that Lybalvi's samidorphan story occupies — two brands now competing for the "weight-friendly antipsychotic" position.

  3. Caplyta approaching $1B annual run rate: At ~$220M quarterly, Caplyta is on track for ~$880M annually. This makes it a larger brand than Alkermes' entire proprietary psychiatry portfolio combined ($346.7M Lybalvi + $370M Aristada + $460M Vivitrol = $1.18B, but Caplyta alone is closing the gap).

Implication for Alkermes: The J&J acquisition fundamentally changes Caplyta's competitive trajectory. A mid-size specialty brand backed by the world's largest healthcare company creates a weight-class mismatch. Alkermes' defense is evidence quality: Lybalvi has 4-year real-world weight data; Caplyta's metabolic claims are based on shorter-term trial data. But J&J's promotional muscle can override evidence advantages through sheer share of voice.

Otsuka/Lundbeck — Abilify Maintena ($420M) + Rexulti ($650M)

  1. Abilify Maintena at ~$420M (+2%), positioning as the "proven LAI": Otsuka's messaging emphasizes longest track record, broadest formulary access, and deepest institutional experience. This is a stability-over-innovation positioning that resonates with risk-averse prescribers.

  2. Rexulti at ~$650M (+8%), AD-agitation approval expanding the franchise: The Alzheimer's agitation indication is bringing new prescribers (neurologists, geriatric psychiatrists) into the Otsuka ecosystem. While not directly competitive with Alkermes' brands, it reinforces Otsuka's dominance in psychiatry mindshare.

Implication for Alkermes: Otsuka's combined franchise (~$1.07B quarterly) continues to dwarf Alkermes' psychiatry portfolio. For Aristada specifically, the competitive dynamics are locked: Abilify Maintena's formulary entrenchment and SOV dominance (40% vs. 20%) won't shift without a structural market event.

Indivior — Sublocade (~$220M, +12%)

  1. Sublocade at ~$220M (+12%), emphasizing "no washout required": Indivior's management commentary explicitly positioned the agonist approach as the emerging standard of care in OUD. "The agonist-first paradigm is becoming standard of care" — a direct challenge to Vivitrol's antagonist mechanism.

  2. Sublocade approaching $850M annual run rate: The growth trajectory, while decelerating from +15% (Q3), remains robust. Sublocade is nearly 2x Vivitrol's annual revenue in OUD-relevant comparisons.

Implication for Alkermes: Indivior's "agonist-first" messaging is the most direct competitive narrative Vivitrol faces. If this framing becomes consensus among addiction medicine specialists, Vivitrol's OUD addressable population shrinks structurally. Vivitrol's AUD indication — where there is no direct Sublocade competition — becomes the brand's primary growth and retention vector.

Teva — Uzedy (~$30M, Slow Growth)

  1. Uzedy growing slowly to ~$30M: Limited dedicated psychiatry promotion from Teva constrains uptake. Concentrated in institutional channels (VA, Medicaid) where formulary committees evaluate total cost of care.

Implication for Alkermes: Uzedy remains a marginal competitor for Aristada. The LAI market is fragmenting (Abilify Maintena, Aristada, Invega, Uzedy) but no single new entrant is disrupting the established share structure.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Alkermes

  1. Cobenfy's Q3→Q4 acceleration ($20M→$50M) is the most important signal this quarter: 150% sequential growth shows BMS's launch execution is working. If this pace continues, Cobenfy reaches Lybalvi-competitive annual revenue within 18 months. The ARISE constraint is holding (monotherapy-only), but BMS is proving that monotherapy switching is commercially viable.

  2. Caplyta under J&J ownership is a larger competitive threat than Cobenfy: At ~$220M quarterly and growing +30%, Caplyta is already 2.5x Cobenfy's revenue. J&J's commercial integration means Caplyta's promotional intensity will increase further. The metabolic positioning overlap with Lybalvi makes this a direct competitive threat, not just a share-of-voice concern.

  3. Alkermes is fighting a budget mismatch that will widen: BMS (Cobenfy) + J&J (Caplyta) + Otsuka (Abilify Maintena/Rexulti) = three large-cap pharma companies investing heavily in the psychiatry space. Alkermes' $1.3B cash position is strong for mid-cap, but its commercial budget cannot match the combined promotional spend of these competitors.

  4. Vivitrol's OUD headwind is structural and earnings-confirmed: Indivior management calling agonist-first "standard of care" is the clearest signal yet that Vivitrol's OUD market is shrinking. Vivitrol's flat guidance ($460-480M for 2026) already bakes this in.

  5. The evidence vs. investment tension is the core Alkermes strategic question: Lybalvi has the best long-term metabolic evidence (4-year data). Cobenfy and Caplyta have bigger marketing budgets. Whether evidence wins over promotion in psychiatry prescribing decisions will determine Lybalvi's growth trajectory in 2026-2027.

5. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Key Evidence This Quarter
1. Cobenfy ARISE failure gives Lybalvi 2-year window UNCHANGED Year-one Cobenfy sales ($155M) and ARISE failure already priced in. Window thesis stable.
2. Aristada SOV stability is deceptive CONFIRMED FY2025 revenue $370M with flat 2026 guidance. Non-recurring GTN favorability of $14M won't recur. True underlying growth is minimal.
3. Vivitrol AUD growth masking OUD share loss NEEDS DATA Full-year revenue ~$460M with management citing AUD growth. PET results should arrive by mid-2025.
4. ZoomRx opportunity is Cobenfy defensive intel UNCHANGED No new signals — waiting on PET wave with Cobenfy tracking.

6. Actions for Vinoth

  • Review Lybalvi PET full wave results when delivered — focus on Cobenfy SoV data and competitive message impact
  • Discuss with Sara Farina: Is the Tactical Optimization proposal ($89,500) moving forward? If not, can it be repositioned as a Cobenfy counter-messaging study?
  • Track Vivitrol PET final report (due June 2025 per SOW) — key data for Thesis 3
  • Note: Lybalvi will overtake Aristada in revenue in 2026 — frame this shift in any account planning conversations
  • Monitor LUMRYZ integration — does it distract Alkermes management attention from psychiatry portfolio?

Vivitrol's AUD indication — where there is no direct Sublocade competition — becomes the brand's primary growth and retention vector.
Teva — Uzedy (~$30M, Slow Growth)

Uzedy growing slowly to ~$30M: Limited dedicated psychiatry promotion from Teva constrains uptake. Concentrated in institutional channels (VA, Medicaid) where formulary committees evaluate total cost of care.

Implication for Alkermes: Uzedy remains a marginal competitor for Aristada. The LAI market is fragmenting (Abilify Maintena, Aristada, Invega, Uzedy) but no single new entrant is disrupting the established share structure.
Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Alkermes


Cobenfy's Q3→Q4 acceleration ($20M→$50M) is the most important signal this quarter: 150% sequential growth shows BMS's launch execution is working. If this pace continues, Cobenfy reaches Lybalvi-competitive annual revenue within 18 months. The ARISE constraint is holding (monotherapy-only), but BMS is proving that monotherapy switching is commercially viable.


Caplyta under J&J ownership is a larger competitive threat than Cobenfy: At ~$220M quarterly and growing +30%, Caplyta is already 2.5x Cobenfy's revenue. J&J's commercial integration means Caplyta's promotional intensity will increase further. The metabolic positioning overlap with Lybalvi makes this a direct competitive threat, not just a share-of-voice concern.


Alkermes is fighting a budget mismatch that will widen: BMS (Cobenfy) + J&J (Caplyta) + Otsuka (Abilify Maintena/Rexulti) = three large-cap pharma companies investing heavily in the psychiatry space. Alkermes' $1.3B cash position is strong for mid-cap, but its commercial budget cannot match the combined promotional spend of these competitors.


Vivitrol's OUD headwind is structural and earnings-confirmed: Indivior management calling agonist-first "standard of care" is the clearest signal yet that Vivitrol's OUD market is shrinking. Vivitrol's flat guidance ($460-480M for 2026) already bakes this in.


The evidence vs. investment tension is the core Alkermes strategic question: Lybalvi has the best long-term metabolic evidence (4-year data). Cobenfy and Caplyta have bigger marketing budgets. Whether evidence wins over promotion in psychiatry prescribing decisions will determine Lybalvi's growth trajectory in 2026-2027.
